perturbation.original_text: This MNDA shall be construed, interpreted and applied in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: This MNDA shall be construed, interpreted and applied in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by the company.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the specification that Massachusetts law governs the agreement. This introduces ambiguity and allows the company to potentially choose a jurisdiction with laws less favorable to the other party, possibly violating consumer protection laws or other regulations that would apply in Massachusetts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, Section 2 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. By removing the governing law clause and replacing it with a vague one, the agreement could be interpreted to circumvent the consumer protections offered under Chapter 93A in Massachusetts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The change from specifying Massachusetts law to 'a jurisdiction chosen by the company' directly contradicts the consumer protection aims of Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, Section 2. This law, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, prohibits 'unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices' in trade. By removing the specific legal jurisdiction, the company could choose a jurisdiction without such protections, potentially circumventing Chapter 93A. This omission weakens the legal obligation to provide fair practices as mandated by Massachusetts law.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this MNDA without cause upon five (5) days written notice given to the other, provided that confidentiality obligations under Section A of this Attachment A shall survive termination hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this MNDA at any time. Upon termination, all obligations cease.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement of written notice and the survival clause for confidentiality obligations. This significantly weakens the protections for confidential information, potentially violating trade secret laws if confidential information is disclosed after termination without the survival clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law protects trade secrets. Removing the survival clause for confidentiality obligations makes it much easier for a party to misappropriate trade secrets without recourse, conflicting with the intent of trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

(2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the survival clause for confidentiality obligations, which contradicts Massachusetts trade secret law. The original text ensured that confidentiality obligations continued even after the MNDA was terminated. By eliminating this clause and stating that 'all obligations cease' upon termination, the modified text allows for the potential misappropriation of trade secrets without legal recourse, directly conflicting with the intent of Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93, Section 42, which defines 'misappropriation' and aims to protect trade secrets by preventing unauthorized disclosure or use. The law snippets define 'improper means' as including 'breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information,' which the original contract protected but the modified contract now fails to protect.

perturbation.original_text: This document and appendices contain the entire agreement between the Parties and supersede any previous oral or written understandings, commitments or agreements pertaining to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This document contains the general understanding between the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'entire agreement' and 'supersede any previous oral or written understandings, commitments or agreements', the contract becomes ambiguous. This omission can invalidate the clause of MNDA because the other party can claim other promises existed orally. This may violate Massachusetts Statute of Frauds, which requires certain agreements to be in writing.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 259, § 1 is the Statute of Frauds, which requires certain contracts, including those that cannot be performed within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. By weakening the 'entire agreement' clause, it implies oral agreements could exist, potentially making the contract unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds if it falls under the types of agreements covered by the statute and is not fully integrated in writing.
perturbation.justification: The original contract includes an integration clause, stating it is the 'entire agreement' and supersedes all prior understandings. The changed text omits this critical clause, stating only that it contains the 'general understanding'. This omission, according to the Perturbation Explanation, invalidates the clause of MNDA. The Law Explanation states that this creates ambiguity regarding oral agreements. Since the Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements to be in writing, removing the integration clause undermines this requirement by implying that oral agreements could exist, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Consistent with other provisions herein, each party assures that it will not knowingly, without obtaining prior authorization from the U.S. Dept. of Commerce Office of Export Administration, transmit directly or indirectly the technical data received pursuant hereto or the immediate product (including processes and services) produced directly by use of such technical data to Afghanistan, People's Republic of China, or any other Country Group Q, S, W, T, or Z country specified in Supplement No. I to Part 370 of U.S. Dept. of Commerce Export Administration Regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will try to comply with export regulations.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit assurance regarding compliance with U.S. export regulations and replaces it with a vague statement of intent. This significantly weakens the commitment to export control, potentially violating federal export control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR requires parties to determine if their export is subject to licensing requirements based on factors like destination, end-use, and end-user. By only agreeing to 'try' to comply and removing the explicit prohibition on exporting to restricted countries without authorization, the contract weakens the obligation to comply with these regulations, potentially violating federal export control laws enforced by the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS).

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly assures compliance with U.S. export regulations, specifically mentioning authorization from the U.S. Dept. of Commerce Office of Export Administration and restrictions on transmitting data to specific countries. The changed text weakens this commitment to merely 'try to comply' with export regulations, which fails to uphold the original obligation. The Law Explanation states that the EAR requires parties to determine licensing requirements. By weakening the obligation, the contract now only suggests a weaker intention which may lead to a failure to comply with regulations, thus creating a contradiction with the EAR. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under Title 15, reinforcing the importance of adhering to these regulations. The omission of specific compliance measures and the replacement with a vague intention directly contradicts the original legally binding commitment.
